coredata.dc:title: Blue room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dot composites with high quantum yield for information encryption and LED light emission
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Shaowen
Zhang, Hongmei
Jian, Ke
Fu, Liming
Lan, Di
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials are crucial for various applications such as information encryption, optoelectronic devices, and biological imaging. However, developing metal-free RTP materials with high quantum yield and long-lived blue luminescence remains a significant challenge. In this study, short-wavelength RTP carbon dots (CDs) composites with high quantum yield in blue and cyan colors were prepared by mixing salicylic acid and boric acid precursors in varying mass ratios and pyrolyzing them at the same temperature. Depending on the amount of salicylic acid, the resulting CDs composites were named CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150), with optimal phosphorescence emission peaks of 400, 400, 455, and 470 nm, respectively. The strategy allowed the luminescent centers of the CDs-x to be confined in a highly rigid boron oxide polycrystalline network and immobilized through the interactions of covalent bonding (C-B) and hydrogen bonding that effectively stabilizes the triplet excited state of CDs-x. As a result, high quantum yield RTP CDs composites were successfully obtained, with the absolute photoluminescence quantum yields up to 95.7 % and phosphorescence quantum yields up to 31.0 %. Moreover, CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150) had high phosphorescence lifetimes of 862.0, 865.3, 826.9, and 731.9 ms, respectively. Meanwhile, with the increase of salicylic acid, the luminescence centers of CDs-x changed from one to two, resulting in a shift in the color of phosphorescence from blue to cyan. These unique luminescent properties make CDs-x composites ideal for excitation-dependent and time-dependent information encryption strategies under different excitations at 254 and 365 nm. Additionally, these composites have great potential in the preparation of light-emitting diodes, showcasing their importance in the field of optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot composites
Blue-cyan room temperature phosphorescence
High quantum yield
Information encryption
LED

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Manufacturing of LED based on CDs-x
Application of CDs-x in information encryption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022ME159) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) TEM, HR-TEM images and histogram of particle size distribution of CDs-25; (d-f) TEM, HR-TEM images and histogram of particle size distribution of CDs-150.
(a-c) XRD spectra, FT-IR spectra and XPS full spectrum of CDs-x; (d-f) XPS fine spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s and B 1 s of CDs-x.

(a-d) RTP spectra of CDs-x; (e-h) Phosphorescence 3D spectra of CDs-x; (i-l) Phosphorescence lifetime decay curves of CDs-x; (m) Fluorescence and phosphorescence pictures of CDs-25 in aqueous solution after switching on and off the 365 nm UV lamp; (n) Luminescence mechanism diagrams of CDs-x composites.

(a-c) Delay emission spectra of CDs-150 as functions of temperature under the excitation wavelength of 250, 280 and 350 nm, respectively. (d-f) 2D pseudo-color contour map of normalized delay emission spectra of CDs-150 as functions of temperature under the excitation wavelength of 250, 280 and 350 nm, respectively. (h).
(a) Encryption process with time-dependent and excitation-dependent encryption; (b) Digital encryption process; (c, d) Colorful message encryption process with time-dependent encryption.

(a) Fabrication schematic, (b) fluorescence spectra under 365 nm excitation, (c) CIE plots corresponding to fluorescence emission peaks, (d) fluorescence spectra under different currents, (e) phosphorescence spectra under 365 nm excitation, and (f) CIE plots corresponding to phosphorescence emission peaks of the CDs-25 based LED elements.
(a) Flowchart of the preparation of CDs-x; (b, c) Fluorescence and phosphorescence optical pictures of CDs-x after switching on and off the UV lamp at 254, 365 nm.

head.ce:title: Blue room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dot composites with high quantum yield for information encryption and LED light emission

Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University of Automotive Technology, Shiyan 442002, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials are crucial for various applications such as information encryption, optoelectronic devices, and biological imaging. However, developing metal-free RTP materials with high quantum yield and long-lived blue luminescence remains a significant challenge. In this study, short-wavelength RTP carbon dots (CDs) composites with high quantum yield in blue and cyan colors were prepared by mixing salicylic acid and boric acid precursors in varying mass ratios and pyrolyzing them at the same temperature. Depending on the amount of salicylic acid, the resulting CDs composites were named CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150), with optimal phosphorescence emission peaks of 400, 400, 455, and 470 nm, respectively. The strategy allowed the luminescent centers of the CDs-x to be confined in a highly rigid boron oxide polycrystalline network and immobilized through the interactions of covalent bonding (C-B) and hydrogen bonding that effectively stabilizes the triplet excited state of CDs-x. As a result, high quantum yield RTP CDs composites were successfully obtained, with the absolute photoluminescence quantum yields up to 95.7 % and phosphorescence quantum yields up to 31.0 %. Moreover, CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150) had high phosphorescence lifetimes of 862.0, 865.3, 826.9, and 731.9 ms, respectively. Meanwhile, with the increase of salicylic acid, the luminescence centers of CDs-x changed from one to two, resulting in a shift in the color of phosphorescence from blue to cyan. These unique luminescent properties make CDs-x composites ideal for excitation-dependent and time-dependent information encryption strategies under different excitations at 254 and 365 nm. Additionally, these composites have great potential in the preparation of light-emitting diodes, showcasing their importance in the field of optoelectronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have a wide range of applications in the fields such as information encryption, low interference bio-imaging, and optical devices due to their unique afterglow luminescence phenomenon [1–8]. While most of the traditional RTP materials are organic RTP materials [9,10]. Although pure organic RTP materials are gaining attentionfor their low cost and various preparation routes. However, in the existing studies, pure organic RTP materials still generally suffer from the disadvantages of high toxicity and short RTP lifetime. And most of the phosphorescent materials do not have high phosphorescence efficiency, making it difficult to simultaneously achieve higher quantum yield and longer lifetime RTP emission [11–14]. The generation of efficient RTP mainly depends on the process of transitioning from the single-linear state (S1) to the triplet state (T1) via intersystem crossing (ISC), as well as the sensitivity of T1 to environmental factors like water and oxygen [15,16]. In order to obtain RTP materials with longer phosphorescence lifetimes, it is common to dope other heteroatoms and introduce carbon-based functional groups to lower the energy barrier of the ISC process while constructing rigid environments to generate more triplet excitons and promote RTP emission [4,17–19]. Among RTP materials, blue materials are particularly important for reducing power consumption in displays and solid-state lighting [20]. However, research on blue RTP materials is relatively difficult due to the inherent long Stoke shift of RTP materials [20]. Therefore, the development of an economical, low-toxicity RTP material with superior afterglow is urgently advocated.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of spherical or quasi-spherical zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm, are an important choice for the construction of RTP materials due to their low cytotoxicity, excellent optical properties, simple synthesis pathways and easy surface functionalization [21]. Usually, it is difficult to achieve long-lived RTP by CDs themselves due to spin prohibition, so researchers have proposed various strategies to improve the RTP performance of CDs, including doping of heteroatoms (B, N, F, etc.) or embedding CDs into rigid substrates (polyvinyl alcohol, boric acid, zeolite, etc.) or immobilizing CDs on the substrate, which, through the effective suppression of molecular rotation, vibration, and non-radiative deactivation induced by intermolecular collisions, could generate and enhance the phosphorescence emission of CDs [22–27]. In recent years, researchers have made many efforts for the development of blue RTP materials. For example, Shi et al. successfully synthesized a novel matrix-free blue phosphorescence-emitting CDs with a phosphorescence lifetime of 315.73 ms and a phosphorescence quantum yield of 12.4 % by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using (3-aminopropyl) trimethoxy silane (APTMS) and phosphoric acid [28]. Sun et al. prepared metal-free CDs with ultra-long RTP lifetimes, excellent stability, as well as an optimal RTP emission of 464 nm in SiO2 by constructing an effective multi-restriction effect using rice husk as a carbon and silicon source [29]. Additionally, Zhang et al. obtained RTP CDs composites with blue to green luminescence and 19.5 % phosphorescence quantum yield by embedding carbonized polymer nanodots synthesized by o-phenylenediamine and poly (acrylic acid) into a rigid immobilized matrix of boron oxide [30]. However, most of the existing blue RTP CDs materials are still less than ideal in terms of phosphorescence quantum yield, phosphorescence lifetime and blue phosphorescence emission range modulation, and the preparation process is complicated with poor reproducibility and stability, which largely limits their development and further practical applications. Therefore, the preparation of blue RTP CDs with simple operation and stable luminescence remains a challenge [31].

Here, blue-cyan colored RTP CDs composites have been successfully synthesized through a one-step pyrolysis reaction of mixtures of salicylic acid (BHA) and boric acid (BA) with varying mass ratios. The CDs were labeled as CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150) based on the amount of BHA used. BA has the advantages of simple structure, good stability, and abundant active sites. In this reaction, BA not only serves as the boron source for CDs, but also forms a rigid matrix of boron oxide (B2O3) after a high-temperature calcination process. The in-situ composition of CDs-x in the B2O3 matrix creates strongly interacting covalent and hydrogen bonds, effectively suppressing nonradiative transitions of triplet excitons caused by molecular vibrations and enhancing the RTP emission of CDs-x. Among them, CDs-25 exhibited the shortest optimal phosphorescence emission wavelength of 400 nm, a phosphorescence lifetime of 865.3 ms, and a phosphorescence quantum yield of 30.1 %. Under 254 nm excitation, CDs-25 displayed a clearly visible and stable 5 s blue RTP. More importantly, the B2O3 matrix provides rigid protection for CDs-25 in aqueous solution, allowing for RTP emission to still occur in aqueous suspension. Furthermore, there are some differences in luminescence performance among the CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150) due to the different mass ratios of BHA to BA. The synthesized CDs-x were successfully utilized in information encryption, based on their distinct phosphorescence decay lifetimes, as well as their excitation-dependent and time-dependent properties. Additionally, light-emitting diodes (LED) were also prepared using CDs-25 as a light-converting layer, demonstrating the feasibility of RTP CDs composites in optoelectronic applications. This study provides a reference for the synthesis of blue RTP materials with high quantum yield and further explores the practical applications of RTP CDs composites.

Salicylic acid (BHA) and boric acid (BA) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ammonium citrate (Ac), cysteine (Cys), ethanol (ALC), acetic acid (HAc), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethyl acetate (EAC) and propylene oxide (PO) were purchased by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents are of analytical purity and were used directly without treatment.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JSM-7800 scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were observed on a JEM-1200EX transmission microscope (JOEL, Japan). X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) were obtained on a Smart Lab 3KW X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a NICOLET 5700 spectrometer with KBr spheres (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing General Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). Phosphorescence spectra, phosphorescence lifetimes, and fluorescence lifetimes were performed on an Edinburgh FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a microsecond flash (μF) and time-selective pass. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) and absolute phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) were obtained directly using Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere at room temperature. The excitation wavelength is 375 nm with excitation power of 5 mW/cm2. Temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectra were conducted with the Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the sample was placed in a low-temperature attachment (Optistat DN, Oxford) and the temperature was controlled between 80 K-298 K.

10, 25, 70 and 150 mg of BHA were added to 3 g of BA, and the mixture of BHA and BA was thoroughly ground at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous white powder mixture. The white powder mixture was placed into a porcelain boat and heated in a muffle furnace at 220 °C for 30 min. After the reaction was finished and cooled to room temperature, the solid sample in the porcelain boat was collected and ground into powder to obtain the CDs-x composites, named CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70 and CDs-150, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Manufacturing of LED based on CDs-x
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the LED devices, 10 mg of CDs-25 was weighed, mixed thoroughly with 10 g of epoxy resin, drop-coated onto a 365 nm GaN chip, and dried at 100 °C for 1 h. The forward operating voltage (Vf) and reverse operating voltage (Vr) of the GaN chip were 3.38 V and 5.00 V, respectively. The LED devices were photoelectrically tested on an HP 9000 (Hangzhou, China) instrument with fluorescence and phosphorescence scans in the ranges of 400–750 nm and 380–750 nm, respectively. Blue emitting LEDs were obtained by using CDs-25 as the color conversion layer. All the measurements were carried out under dark conditions.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 a, CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150) composites were successfully prepared by heating different mass ratios of BHA (10, 25, 70, 150 mg) and BA (3 g) at 220 °C for 30 min. Under 254 and 365 nm UV light excitation, CDs-x exhibited bright blue-cyan fluorescence, with the luminescence lasting for approximately 5.0 s at room temperature after the excitation light source was turned off. More interestingly, they showed RTP emission that was almost consistent with the fluorescence color (Scheme 1b, c). Initially, 10 sets of data were designed for the reaction between 0–150 mg BHA and 3 g BA to obtain 10 sets of RTP CDs composites. In order to select representative CDs materials and explore their luminescence properties, fluorescence and phosphorescence spectroscopy tests were conducted on CDs-x (Fig. S1, S2, and S3). These tests compared the optimal excitation, optimal emission, and fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities at specific excitation wavelengths of 254 and 365 nm, respectively. Moreover, the positions of the best emission peaks of fluorescence and phosphorescence were plotted on CIE coordinate charts to visualize their luminescence colors. With the increase of BHA dosage, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs composites changed from single-peak to double-peak emission, with the color adjusting from blue to cyan and the luminescence centers of CDs undergoing changes. Ultimately, four CDs-x composites were selected based on their higher luminescence intensity and more pronounced changes in emission peaks: CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150. Their morphologies and chemical structures were characterized in detail to understand the reasons for the differences in luminescence and the origins of RTP.

The surface groups of the CDs composites were further investigated using FT-IR analysis. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2b) of CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70 and CDs-150 show significant absorption peaks near 3200, 1415, and 1195 cm−1, which can be attributed to O-H, B-O and B-O-C stretching vibrations, respectively. Additionally, a weak absorption band at 960 cm−1 could be observed, indicating the presence of C-B stretching vibration. This suggests that the CDs-x and BA successfully combine through a covalent coupling reaction, due to the electron-deficient nature of the B atoms and the amphiphilic nature of the C atoms [34]. The results of FT-IR analysis also indicate that B and O elements are doped into the CDs, and a large amount of B2O3 is present in the composites. Furthermore, the standard O-H stretching vibration peak is usually located between 3650 cm−1 − 3600 cm−1. However, in Fig. 2b, the peak of the stretching vibration of –OH is shifted obviously to a lower wavenumber, which can be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonding. This averaging out of the electron cloud density reduces the frequency of the stretching vibration of –OH. To further prove the hydrogen bonding between the CDs and the BA matrix, temperature-dependent FT-IR spectra of CDs-150 were investigated. As the temperature increased, the O-H peaks were redshifted and broadened (Fig. S5), confirming the formation of hydrogen bonds between the CDs and BA matrix [35,36]. The aforementioned results further suggest that the CDs are successfully immobilized in the B2O3 matrix through a combination of hydrogen bonding and covalent bonding (C-B) [8].

Subsequently, the chemical structural composition of the samples was analyzed by performing XPS tests on CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150). The full XPS spectra revealed the presence of elements C, O, and B in CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150 (Fig. 2c). The characteristic peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s, and B 1 s were observed at 285, 533 and 193 eV, respectively. Table S1 shows the elemental contents of C, O, and B in each sample, with CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150 containing 13.3, 68.6, 18.1 %; 15.1, 67.3, 17.6 %; 18.4, 66.8, 14.8 %; and 19.7, 66.4, 13.9 % of each element, respectively. As the BHA dosage increased, the C content increased while the B content decreased. Further analysis of the C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2d) of CDs-x showed four binding energy peaks of CDs-x around 284.0, 284.9, 286.3, and 288.5 eV, corresponding to C-B, C-C/C = C, C-O, and C = O, respectively. The O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e) indicated that elemental O was primarily present in the form of C = O (532.9 eV) and C-O (533.6 eV). The B 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2f) confirmed the presence of BCO2 (192.3 eV), B2O3 (193.1 eV) and B-O (194.2 eV). In addition, the absorption peak at 192.3 eV suggested the oxidation of B atoms [37], further supporting the presence of boron oxide in the XRD spectra. The proportions of subpeaks in the CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70 and CDs-150 composites (Table S2) display an increasing level of BCO2 bond, indicating that CDs and BA are dehydrated and condensed at high temperatures, forming covalent bonds [35], which may be a significant factor in the differences observed in the luminescence properties of CDs-x. The results of both XPS and FT-IR analyses were consistent, indicating that the high-temperature calcination of a homogeneous mixture of BHA and BA formed stable C-B covalent and hydrogen bonds, resulting in a highly rigid B2O3 polycrystalline network with spatial network restriction in the outer layer [37]. This limited the luminescent centers of CDs-x to a rigid environment, suppressing their nonradiative transitions and ultimately generating the RTP emission [19,29].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. S6) show that the CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150 composites have a distinct absorption peak near 250 nm attributed to the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structural domain. There is a broad absorption band at 280–500 nm, which is attributed to the n → π* transition of C = O. The presence of n → π* transitions facilitates the transition of singlet state to the triplet state[38,39]. When both π → π* and n-π* transitions are present in the molecule, these two states are strongly mixed by vertical vibrations between the planes, which can increase the rate of ISC and lead to the rapid generation of the triplet state, thus realizing effective phosphorescence emission[40]. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150 at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S7a-d) show that the optimal excitation wavelengths are 370 nm for CDs-10 and CDs-25, and 410 nm for CDs-70 and CDs-150. The composites of the four CDs have two fluorescence emission peaks, with the positions of the peaks remaining constant regardless of the excitation wavelength, demonstrating excitation independence. The two emission peaks of CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150 appear at 412, 505 nm, 418, 515 nm, 460, 518 nm, and 470, 529 nm, respectively. With an increase in BHA dosage, the degree of oxidation of CDs-x is enhanced, resulting in a gradual intensification of the emission peaks at longer wavelengths compared with the emission peaks at shorter wavelengths and a shift in fluorescence color from blue to cyan. The phosphorescence spectra and corresponding 3D spectra (Fig. 3-d, e-h) show that the optimal excitation wavelength for phosphorescence is 310 nm for CDs-10, and 290 nm for CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150. CDs-10 and CDs-25 have broad single-peak emission with optimal emission wavelengths both at 400 nm, accompanied by a shoulder peak at 450 nm. In contrast, the phosphorescence emission of CDs-70 and CDs-150 exhibits more significant bimodal emission. Notably, CDs-70 presents two emission peaks at 403 and 455 nm with comparable intensity. Comparatively, CDs-150 has two emission peaks at 403 and 470 nm, with the intensity of the emission peak at 470 nm being much higher than that at 403 nm. The positions of these emission peaks correspond to the blue-cyan emission color. CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150 have high quantum yields compared to the reported RTP CDs composites (Table S3 and Table S4). Their absolute photoluminescence quantum yields (QY) were measured to be 95.7, 71.7, 61.5, and 59.0 %, respectively, and their phosphorescence quantum yields (PQY) were 23.1, 30.1, 31.0, and 7.5 %, respectively, at the optimal excitation wavelength. They also have long phosphorescence lifetimes of 862.0, 865.3, 826.9, and 731.9 ms (Fig. 3 i-l), and fluorescence lifetimes of 10.7, 12.4, 8.0, and 7.4 ns (Fig. S8a-d), respectively.

Triplet-state excitons are unstable and highly prone to quenching by water and oxygen in the environment. However, surprisingly, the CDs-x composites showed excellent stability. The afterglow intensity of the CDs-x exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp for 14 d remained more than 80 % of the initial intensity, indicating excellent anti-photobleaching properties (Fig. S9). The afterglow intensity of the CDs-x remained more than 80 % of the initial intensity even placed in an oven at 80 °C for 7 d, exhibiting their excellent thermal stability (Fig. S10). In addition, these composites also showed excellent resistance to being quenched by water. As in Fig. 3i, the suspended aqueous solution of CDs-25 emits blue fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm UV light, and has a blue RTP emission clearly visible to the naked eye for 1 s after the UV light is turned off. To further explore the stability of the CDs-x, CDs-25 as typical sample was dispersed in solvents with different polarities (ALC, HAc, DMSO, DMF, EAC and PO), as well as in strong oxidizing agents (30 wt% H2O2 and 98 wt% H2SO4). The fluorescence and RTP emission remained visible to the naked eye at 365 nm UV light (Fig. S11). This renders them appropriate for a wide range of applications, even in tough environments. Based on our characterization and test results, the luminescence mechanism of CDs-x was further investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 3j. The radiative transition in CDs-x occurs when the electrons absorb energy from the incident light and transition from the ground state (S0) to the excited single line state (S1). A portion of the S1 exciton then transitions to the triplet state (T1) through ISC, and eventually returns to the ground state through a radiative transition, resulting in phosphorescence [41,42]. However, the presence of a spin potential barrier between S1 and T1, combined with the susceptibility of electrons to environmental factors such as oxygen and water, often leads to non-radiative transitions at T1, making it challenging to achieve RTP emission [35]. Nevertheless, in the synthesis of CDs-x composites, the incorporation of BHA and BA within a structure composed of numerous covalent and hydrogen bonds, along with the heat treatment of BA to create a highly rigid B2O3 polycrystalline network, serves to confine and immobilize the luminescent centers of CDs-x, effectively suppressing non-radiative transitions of T1 excitons [43,44]. Additionally, the FT-IR results confirm the successful doping of B and O into CDs-x. The interaction between the n-π* transitions of C = O and the π-π* transitions of C = C promotes the ISC process [19]. Furthermore, the introduction of B atoms, which are electronegative, reduces the energy gap between S1 and T1, accelerating the generation of T1 excitons and promoting RTP emission, resulting in a high phosphorescence quantum yield and a long phosphorescence lifetime of CDs-x composites [4]. Even in aqueous solution, the covalent and hydrogen bonding and the protective effect of the B2O3 rigid matrix allow for RTP emission in CDs-x composites [43,44]. However, the presence of environmental factors such as water and oxygen can hinder the ISC process and stability of T1 excitons, leading to non-radiative transitions due to vibrational relaxation and internal energy conversion, resulting in a shorter duration and weaker intensity of RTP emission for CDs-x in aqueous solution compared to solid-state composites[45]. Based on the above discussion, it is believed that extremely high stability of CDs-x composites is ascribe to the multiple confinement interactions of covalent bonding, hydrogen bonding and physical immobilization [46].

In order to further explore the origins of each peak. Pure BA without adding BHA and pure BHA without adding BA were calcined under the same conditions with CDs-x, the obtained materials were referred to Pure-BA and CDs-BHA, respectively. The BA calcined product is also the host matrix of CDs-x. Although, very weak RTP of Pure-BA at 400 nm can be observed compared to that of CDs-x (Fig. S12, Fig. S13), which exactly corresponds to the delayed emission peak of CDs-x at 400 nm. It is believed delayed emission peak of CDs-x at 400 nm may be come from the host matrix. Additionally, No-RTP was observed when BHA was used alone as a precursor. But, with the increase of BHA dosage, more and more CDs are generated in CDs-x, resulting in a new peak appearing around 450–470 nm and gradually strengthening and dominating (Fig. 3a-d, Fig. S12), further confirming delayed emission peak of CDs-x at 450–470 nm mainly come from the formed CDs. Therefore, the high-efficiency delayed luminescence of CDs-x comes from the synergistic effect of BA and CDs. We speculate that, firstly, during the carbonization, BHA was polymerized via C═C bonds and dehydrated and crosslinked with BA via carboxyl group and hydroxyl group, forming B, O-doped CDs [47]. The UV–vis spectra in Fig. S14 show that BHA has two characteristic absorption peaks at 230 nm and 300 nm. However, these two peaks disappear in the CDs-x, further demonstrating that BHA has reacted completely. Secondly, BA is crucial for the production of RTP. BA not only crosslinks with BHA to form covalent bonds and hydrogen bonds, but also forms a rigid B2O3 matrix, resulting in CDs being confined in a rigid environment (Fig. 4 j). This is beneficial for hindering the quenching of excited triplet excitons and thereby facilitating RTP emission [48,49]. To further investigate the role of the BA matrix for the luminescence of CDs-x, CDs-70 powders were dissolved in water and then dried and crystallized naturally to get CDs-70–0. This process undermines the original strong confinement interactions between the CDs and BA. Consequently, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs-70–0 is markedly lower than that of CDs-70 (Fig. S15). Additionally, control experiment with different BA dosage were performed. BA-x were prepared from BHA (70 mg) and BA (200, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500 mg). As shown in Fig. S16, it can be seen that with lower BA added (200–500 mg), the delay emission is not only very weak, but also single peak at 470 nm, with the BA dosage increasing, accompanied by the appearance of a new emission peak at 400 nm, a bimodal emission is presented and continuously enhanced. The results further confirm our above speculation about the origins of the two delayed emission peak of CDs-x, one is from BA matrix, the other is mainly from the formed CDs embedded in BA matrix, and the synergistic effect of BA and CDs promote the luminescence of CDs-x.

To further confirm the afterglow mechanism of CD-x, the variable temperature tests were carried out. As shown in Fig. 4a-f, under 250 nm, 280 nm and 350 nm excitation, the delay emission of CDs-150 present similar trends with the temperature increasing (80 K-298 K), that is, the intensity of delay emission at 400 nm decreases firstly with the temperature (80 K-260 K) and then increases (260 K-298 K), while the intensity at 450 nm emission continuously decreases with the temperature increasing (80 K-298 K). We supposed that the short-wavelength at 400 nm more likely to be attributed to the thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) and the long-wavelength at 470 nm is attributed to the phosphorescence [50]. Under 250 nm excited, TADF at 400 nm is relatively strong (Fig. 4g), which is consistent to the host matrix luminescence of Pure-BA (Fig. S13), further suggesting the TADF at 400 nm mainly originates from BA matrix. Compared to the delay emission spectra excited at 250 nm, the TADF emission at 400 nm is relatively weaker under 350 nm excitation, while phosphorescence emission at 470 nm enhances and dominates the entire spectrum (Fig. 4h), which is consistent to the luminescence of CDs (Fig. S17), further confirming the longer wavelength phosphorescence originates from the CDs.

Intensity changing trend of 400 nm peak of CDs-150 as a function of temperature under 250 excitation (black line) and 350 nm excitation (blue line). (i) Intensity changing trend of 470 nm peak of CDs-150 as a function of temperature under 250 excitation (black line) and 350 nm excitation (blue line). (g) Schematic diagram of possible structure of CDs-x.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs-x in information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-x composites have a broad potential for application in the field of information encryption, thanks to their similar colors and varying phosphorescence lifetimes. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the powders of CDs-10, CDs-25, CDs-70, and CDs-150 were affixed onto branches, trunks, letters, and foliage parts of a tree with solid glue sticks as per the designed pattern. Under 254 and 365 nm excitation, the pattern emitted a blue fluorescence, which continued to produce RTP emission for 4 s after the UV light was turned off. After removing the 365 nm excitation, the pattern gradually disappeared in the following order: letters, leaves and flowers, trunk, and branches, respectively, due to the attenuation of phosphorescence. Similarly, when the 254 nm excitation was removed, the pattern at 0.5 s remained consistent with that of 365 nm, while the patterns at 2, 3, and 4 s each conveyed different information. This successful design of a multidimensional information encryption strategy incorporates both excitation and time dependence.

Next, a digital encryption strategy was designed using CDs-25 and CDs-150, as shown in Fig. 5b. These two composites have the largest difference in phosphorescence lifetimes and are better able to capture valid information as time changes. According to the designed digital code, CDs-25 and CDs-150 were fixed at the corresponding positions. When the 254 nm UV lamp was turned on, the digits showed blue fluorescence, conveying the error message “8888″. When the UV lamp was turned off, the digits showed cyan RTP for 4 s, also conveying the error message ”8888″. However, at the 4th second of RTP emission, the real message “HOPE” was successfully displayed in blue RTP. This encryption strategy presents misleading error information at the moment of UV lamp switching on and off, and the valid information can only be observed at a specific time (4th second). When the 254 nm UV lamp was turned on, the digits showed blue fluorescence, conveying the error message “8888″. When the UV lamp was turned off, the digits showed cyan RTP for 4 s, also conveying the error message ”8888″. However, at the 4th second of RTP emission, the real message “HOPE” was successfully displayed finally in blue RTP. This encryption strategy presents misleading error information at the moment of UV lamp switching on and off, and the valid information can only be observed at a specific time (4th second). This numerical encryption strategy, with a final delivery of a word, is different from the usual decryption idea and improves the security of information encryption and delivery. Furthermore, the method of preparing CDs-x composites mentioned in this experiment is universal and applicable. To increase the color options for the patterns, under the same experimental conditions, CDs-Ac and CDs-Cys composites were successfully prepared with green and red RTP emission, respectively, using 25 mg of ammonium citrate (Ac) and 25 mg of cysteine (Cys) after a pyrolysis reaction with 3 g of BA, and applied them to the message encryption along with CDs-25 and CDs-150. In Fig. 5c, CDs-Cys, CDs-Ac, CDs-25, and CDs-150 represent flowers, leaves, branches of the tree, and letters, respectively. They exhibit red, green, blue, and cyan fluorescence under 254 nm excitation, and after the UV lamp is removed, RTPs consistent with the color of the fluorescence are produced, and with time, at 0.1, 1.5, 3.0, and 4.5 s respectively, different patterns are delivered, with the final pattern being the branches of the tree represented by CDs-25. In Fig. 5d, CDs-25, CDs-Cys, and CDs-Ac denote butterflies, flowers, and leaves, respectively. Similarly, as time decays, a different pattern is presented each second, with the final message delivered being the four butterflies denoted by CDs-25. These information encryption strategies demonstrate the successful design of multidimensional information encryption processes using the luminescent colors, phosphorescence lifetimes, and RTP decay rates at different excitation wavelengths of CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150, Ac, Cys). The synthesized CDs composites have great potential for real-world information encryption and delivery applications.

Blue light-emitting diodes (LEDs) (<450 nm) are essential in light sources such as solid-state lighting, vivid displays and high-density information storage. The high quantum yield of CDs-x makes them ideal for use in LEDs. Taking CDs-25 with high quantum yield and long afterglow lifetime as an example, the optimal excitation wavelength for fluorescence of CDs-25 is 370 nm. Therefore, when preparing the LED device, we choose 365 nm commercial GaN substrate as the excitation light source and CDs-25 as the optical conversion material. The fabrication process of the LEDs is illustrated in Fig. 6 a. Fig. 6b shows that when energized, the CDs-25 LED (LED-25) emits blue fluorescence, with the optimal emission occurring at 400 nm. This corresponds to an optimal color coordinate of (0.18, 0.15) (Fig. 6c). The emission position of LED-25 remains consistent at different current intensities (20–120 mA), indicating the significant stability of the blue emission (Fig. 6d). Most notably, LED-25 can display a blue RTP for 4 s after power off (Fig. 6e), with a phosphorescence emission center of 450 nm, corresponding to the CIE coordinate of (0.18, 0.22) (Fig. 6f), demonstrating good luminescence and color rendering properties. The study of such CDs for optoelectronic devices presents the potential for next-generation carbon photonics and optoelectronics applications.

In summary, blue-cyan RTP CDs composites with high quantum yield and long phosphorescence lifetime were successfully prepared by heat treating BHA and BA with different mass ratios in a single step. This presents a simple and efficient strategy for the preparation of RTP CDs composites with excellent optical properties. By controlling the mass ratio of the reactants, the performance of CDs-x (x = 10, 25, 70, 150) can be easily tuned. These composites have QYs of 95.7, 71.7, 61.5, and 59.0 %, PQYs as high as 23.1, 30.1, 31.0, and 7.5 %, and long phosphorescence lifetimes of 862.0, 865.3, 826.9 and 731.9 ms, with a naked-eye observation time of approximately 5 s. The excellent RTP performance of CDs-x is attributed to the spatial confinement of the rigid matrix of B2O3 and the interactions of B-C covalent and hydrogen bonds. These factors promote the ISC process and effectively inhibit the non-radiative deactivation of the triplet state, resulting in efficient RTP emission. Additionally, the rigid matrix of B2O3 is not easily destroyed in aqueous solution, allowing CDs-25 to maintain RTP emission in this environment. Furthermore, the developed CDs-x composites have similar luminescence color and adjustable afterglow length making them suitable for applications in information encryption. These composites can be used with time-resolved and excitation-dependent information encryption strategies, increasing the difficulty of decryption and providing higher security for information transmission. Moreover, CDs-x composites have potential applications in the preparation of blue RTP LEDs, highlighting their broad potential in the fields of information encryption and optoelectronic devices.

Shaowen Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hongmei Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ke Jian: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Liming Fu: Formal analysis, Data curation. Di Lan: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xihui Zhao: Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022ME159) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.157119.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activating Room Temperature Long Afterglow of Carbon Dots via Covalent Fixation
